title: Sourcegraph Enterprise tokens should not be disclosed
description: In most cases, trust boundaries are violated when a secret is exposed in a source code repository or an uncontrolled deployment environment. Unintended people who don’t need to know the secret might get access to it. They might then be able to use it to gain unwanted access to associated services or resources.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

checks.message: Sourcegraph Enterprise token detected in source code. Sourcegraph Enterprise tokens (sge_ prefix) provide privileged administrative and enterprise-level API access to self-hosted Sourcegraph instances, enabling comprehensive enterprise management, security, and governance operations. These tokens grant extensive permissions including: managing site-wide configuration and settings, controlling user authentication and authorization systems, managing enterprise licenses and subscription settings, configuring SSO/SAML and external authentication providers, managing organization-wide security policies, accessing and modifying all repositories across the instance, managing code host connections and integrations, configuring enterprise batch changes and campaigns, accessing site-level analytics and usage metrics, managing extensions and extension registry, controlling rate limits and resource quotas, managing database and storage configurations, accessing and modifying site admin settings, managing outbound webhooks and integrations, controlling feature flags and experimental features, managing background jobs and worker processes, accessing system health and monitoring data, managing user accounts and permissions across the enterprise, configuring code intelligence and precise indexing, managing external services and API integrations, controlling search performance and indexing, managing audit logs and compliance reporting, configuring permissions synchronization, and managing site-level security scanning. Exposing Sourcegraph Enterprise tokens enables attackers to: gain administrative control over entire Sourcegraph instance affecting all users and repositories, access all source code repositories in the enterprise deployment, modify authentication systems creating backdoor administrator accounts, disable security controls and audit logging, exfiltrate enterprise-wide code intelligence data, manipulate license and subscription settings, access and modify all user accounts and permissions, inject malicious code through batch changes affecting entire codebase, access sensitive configuration including database credentials and API keys, disable SSO/SAML breaking authentication for all users, modify external service integrations exposing connected systems, access comprehensive audit logs revealing security posture, manipulate rate limits causing denial of service, access system metrics revealing infrastructure details, modify code host connections affecting version control integration, delete or corrupt repositories across the instance, access enterprise analytics revealing usage patterns, manipulate extensions introducing malicious plugins, and pivot to underlying infrastructure through configuration access. This is a critical security vulnerability requiring immediate remediation. Take immediate action: revoke the exposed enterprise token immediately from site admin settings (Site admin → Access tokens), generate a new enterprise token with minimal necessary administrative scopes, update all administrative scripts, monitoring tools, and automation with the new token, conduct comprehensive audit of instance access logs for unauthorized administrative actions, review all configuration changes made during exposure period, verify no backdoor administrator accounts were created, check for unauthorized repository access or modifications, review all batch changes and campaigns for malicious modifications, audit external service configurations for tampering, verify authentication provider configurations remain intact, review user account changes and permission modifications, check for unauthorized extensions or plugin installations, verify database and storage configurations remain secure, audit webhook and integration configurations for unauthorized endpoints, implement principle of least privilege for enterprise tokens (avoid site-admin level when possible), store enterprise tokens in dedicated enterprise secret management systems with strict access controls, use automation service accounts with minimal required permissions, never commit enterprise tokens to any repository including internal tools, implement comprehensive secret scanning across all repositories and configurations, rotate enterprise tokens on strict schedule (every 30-60 days for administrative tokens), require MFA for all accounts with enterprise token creation privileges, implement IP whitelisting for administrative API access, monitor and alert on all enterprise token usage and administrative actions, maintain detailed inventory of all enterprise tokens with purpose and ownership, implement break-glass procedures for emergency administrative access, segregate duties between different administrative functions, regularly audit enterprise token permissions and access patterns, educate administrators about enterprise token security and proper handling procedures, integrate enterprise token detection in infrastructure-as-code scanning, implement approval workflows for enterprise token creation and renewal, and conduct regular security reviews of administrative access controls and token usage patterns.
